title: Frozen Out: How the United States Forgot Its Far North
content:
When U.S. President Donald Trump arrived at Joint Base Elmendorf-Richardson to meet with Russian President Vladimir Putin, political pundits took notice of the symbolic venue. Alaska, a former Russian territory sold to the United States, represents both a historic and geographic bridge between the two powers. Siberia lies just 55 miles away across the Bering Strait, and many Alaska Natives share more in common with the region’s Indigenous Chukchi people than with the average American. Yet the sea ice, remote villages, and snowcapped mountains that define the Last Frontier were far removed from the president's visit. A low-hanging cloud bank likely kept him from even glimpsing the summit of Mount McKinley before returning to the Lower 48.

Since taking office in 2017, Trump has made very few visits to this remote outpost. He had last traveled to the state in 2022 for a rally ahead of his reelection bid, and previous trips were merely refueling stopovers on flights to and from Asia. In total, Trump has made just five official visits to Alaska, compared with 21 to Florida in 2025 alone. This seeming indifference to the 49th state fits comfortably with historic U.S. attitudes toward the far north. In fact, Barack Obama remains the only sitting U.S. president to have ever set foot above the Arctic Circle, despite the rapid and destabilizing warming of the region.

Such political detachment largely defines the history of U.S. engagement in the Arctic. After the Alaska Purchase, there were long-held fears that the territory would fall into British hands, which initially motivated Russia to quickly offload the icy land. The deal finally proved its immense worth in 1896 when gold was discovered in Canada’s nearby Klondike region, kicking off a massive gold rush. However, it was not until World War II that Washington fully understood the strategic value of the purchase. Alaska served as a vital buffer zone and a critical pathway for transferring aircraft to the Soviet Union during the conflict.

The strategic importance of Alaska was recognized by some military leaders well before it became a political priority. In 1935, General Billy Mitchell, a pioneer of the U.S. Air Force, famously told Congress that whoever holds Alaska will hold the world. He strongly emphasized the territory’s unmatched value as a northern air route and a crucial defensive position. Despite these early warnings, modern political engagement often fails to reflect the region's true geopolitical significance. As the Arctic continues to change rapidly, the United States must reevaluate its commitment to this vital northern frontier.
